subject: english
learning_objectives: Define an idiom and explain how it differs from literal language
Learn the meanings of common English idioms

sections.heading: Definition
sections.content: An idiom is an expression whose meaning cannot be inferred from the meanings of its individual words. For example, 'He shed crocodile tears' does not mean he literally cried like a crocodile – it means he cried insincerely.

sections.heading: Common Idioms and Their Meanings
sections.content:
To chase one's shadow – to deceive oneself
To add insult to injury – to worsen an already bad situation
To give an airy nothing – to make empty, useless remarks
An acid test – a very severe test
Achilles' heel / The heel of Achilles – one's weak spot or weakness
An ample opportunity – many or various opportunities
To play to the gallery – to seek cheap popularity
To throw light – to explain or clarify
To have a light finger – to be inclined to steal
To breathe one's last – to die
To be armed to the teeth – to be fully equipped
An apple of discord – a source of disagreement or conflict
To be at crossroads – to be at a critical point in life or career
To be at sixes and sevens – to be in utter confusion or chaos
Bed of roses – a comfortable, pleasant situation
Baptism of fire – a very difficult first experience
To beat about the bush – to be indirect and vague
Beginning of a new era – a historic turning point
To be in the same boat – to share the same situation as someone else
To blaze a/the trail – to be the first to do something
To be a bird of a feather – to be alike (note: NOT 'birds of the same feather')
To blow one's trumpet – to boast or praise oneself
To burn the candle at both ends – to overwork by working early and late
To burn the midnight oil – to study or work late into the night
To bury the hatchet – to end a quarrel and make peace
By rule of thumb – in an unscientific, approximate way
To call to a halt – to stop something
Captain of industry – a controller or manager of a large company
By hook or by crook – by any means possible
To bring someone/something to their knees – to humble or defeat
To build castles in the air – to have unrealistic hopes
To be tied to one's apron strings – to be under someone's control
To be at a loose end – to be idle or have nothing to do
To draw a line – to set a limit
To be behind closed doors – to act secretly or privately
To nip a problem in the bud – to stop a problem early before it grows
To hold an olive branch – to offer peace
A snail's pace – very slowly
A wet blanket – a person who discourages others
A stone's throw – a very short distance
A backlog – an accumulation of unfinished work; arrears
To kill someone in cold blood – to kill deliberately without emotion
To be at low ebb – to be in a poor or bad state
To have firsthand information – to have information directly from the source
To catch one's eye – to attract someone's attention
To take the bull by the horn – to face a difficult situation boldly
To live from hand to mouth – to have no savings; to barely survive
To water down – to weaken or dilute
